old_value: {"id":"compress-claude-bn9","title":"CLI examples for all compression formats","description":"Create CLI examples demonstrating all three layers (L1 pure, L2 async, L3 sync) for each compression format: flate, gzip, zlib, lzw, bzip2, and checksum. Examples live in lib/examples/ with moon.pkg configs.","acceptance_criteria":"All examples compile and run. L1/L3 examples work on wasm-gc, L2 async examples work on native target with #cfg guards.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T02:53:11Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T02:53:11Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-2tv","title":"Optimize CRC-32 with slicing-by-8","description":"CRC-32 is 427x slower than Go because Go uses hardware SSE4.2/CLMUL instructions. Since MoonBit/WASM can't use hardware CRC, implement slicing-by-8 (process 8 bytes per iteration with 8 precomputed tables) to get ~8x improvement over current byte-at-a-time approach.

**Current**: 110.9 µs/10KB (byte-at-a-time table lookup)
**Go**: 0.26 µs/10KB (hardware CRC)
**Target**: ~14 µs/10KB (slicing-by-8, still ~54x slower than hardware but 8x better)","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Implement 8-table slicing-by-8 CRC-32
- [ ] CRC-32 benchmark improves by at least 5x
- [ ] All checksum tests still pass","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T02:32:50Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T02:32:50Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-2tv","title":"Optimize CRC-32 with slicing-by-8","description":"CRC-32 is 427x slower than Go because Go uses hardware SSE4.2/CLMUL instructions. Since MoonBit/WASM can't use hardware CRC, implement slicing-by-8 (process 8 bytes per iteration with 8 precomputed tables) to get ~8x improvement over current byte-at-a-time approach.

**Current**: 110.9 µs/10KB (byte-at-a-time table lookup)
**Go**: 0.26 µs/10KB (hardware CRC)
**Target**: ~14 µs/10KB (slicing-by-8, still ~54x slower than hardware but 8x better)","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Implement 8-table slicing-by-8 CRC-32
- [ ] CRC-32 benchmark improves by at least 5x

{"id":"compress-claude-2jw","title":"Optimize Adler-32 with SWAR/block processing","description":"Adler-32 is 45x slower than Go. Implement block processing (NMAX=5552 chunk optimization) and potentially SWAR tricks to reduce overhead.

**Current**: 97.4 µs/10KB
**Go**: 2.15 µs/10KB","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Adler-32 benchmark improves by at least 3x
- [ ] All checksum tests still pass","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T02:33:00Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T02:33:00Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-taf","title":"Optimize Huffman decoder with lookup tables","description":"The current Huffman decoder in flate/decompress decodes one bit at a time. Go uses a two-level table lookup (chunks + links) that decodes multiple bits per lookup. This is the #1 bottleneck — decompression is 49x slower than Go.

**Current**: bit-by-bit tree traversal in `HuffmanDecoder::decode`
**Target**: single-lookup for codes ≤9 bits, two-level for longer codes
**Impact**: Should bring decompression from ~49x to ~5-10x slower than Go","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Huffman decoder uses precomputed lookup table (≥9-bit first level)
- [ ] flate decompress benchmark improves by at least 5x
- [ ] All existing tests still pass","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T02:32:43Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T02:32:43Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-taf","title":"Optimize Huffman decoder with lookup tables","description":"The current Huffman decoder in flate/decompress decodes one bit at a time. Go uses a two-level table lookup (chunks + links) that decodes multiple bits per lookup. This is the #1 bottleneck — decompression is 49x slower than Go.

**Current**: bit-by-bit tree traversal in `HuffmanDecoder::decode`
**Target**: single-lookup for codes ≤9 bits, two-level for longer codes
**Impact**: Should bring decompression from ~49x to ~5-10x slower than Go","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Huffman decoder uses precomputed lookup table (≥9-bit first level)
- [ ] flate decompress benchmark improves by at least 5x

{"id":"compress-claude-rlq","title":"Optimize DEFLATE compressor","description":"DEFLATE compression is 2-6x slower than Go depending on data type. Key areas:

- Token encoding efficiency
- Lazy matching for levels 4-9
- Random data is 6x slower (worst case)","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] flate compress benchmarks improve by at least 2x

{"id":"compress-claude-a8g","title":"Eliminate Ref[T] indirection in inflate hot path","description":"The inflate decompression loop uses Ref[UInt64] and Ref[Int] for bit_buf, bit_count, and pos. Each access goes through heap-allocated Ref indirection. Convert to a struct with mutable fields passed directly, or restructure to avoid Ref entirely. Benchmarks show 12-57x slower than Go decompressor.","acceptance_criteria":"Ref[T] removed from decode_huffman_block hot path. Decompress benchmark improves measurably. All tests pass.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:22Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:22Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-6xa","title":"Dynamic Huffman block encoding for compressor","description":"Currently the compressor only emits fixed Huffman blocks. Dynamic Huffman blocks with custom code tables tuned to the actual symbol frequencies produce significantly smaller output, especially for larger inputs. Go's compressor uses dynamic blocks by default. This affects compression ratio more than speed.","acceptance_criteria":"Compressor emits dynamic Huffman blocks when they produce smaller output than fixed blocks. Compression ratio improves on text data. All tests pass.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:35Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:35Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-2qx","title":"Level-differentiated DEFLATE compression strategies","description":"Currently all compression levels 1-9 use the same LZ77 algorithm with only chain length varying. Go uses completely different strategies per level (e.g. snappy-like for level 1, lazy matching for 4-6, optimal parsing for 7-9). Implement at least: (1) fast token-based encoder for level 1 without hash chains, (2) lazy matching for levels 4+. Compression is 1.3-4x slower than Go at 10KB.","acceptance_criteria":"BestSpeed (level 1) compress benchmark improves by at least 30%. Compression ratio at DefaultCompression stays within 5% of current. All tests pass.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:29Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:29Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"The Array[Token] used to collect LZ77 output heap-allocates for every literal byte and match. For 10KB input this means ~10K allocations minimum. This is the primary cause of the 35-62% compression speed regression.

Preferred approach: use #valtype with bit-packed Int64 to eliminate ALL heap allocation per token.

```moonbit
#valtype
struct PackedToken {
  val : Int64
}
```

Encoding scheme:
- Literal: val = byte_value (0-255, bit 32 clear)
- Match: val = (length << 16) | dist | (1 << 32) (bit 32 set = match flag)

This gives zero GC pressure since #valtype structs are stored inline/on-stack, not heap-allocated. See https://www.moonbitlang.com/blog/moonbit-value-type

Combined with a pre-allocated FixedArray[PackedToken] (data.length() is upper bound on token count), this should recover all the regression from the two-pass approach.

Previous options (still valid fallbacks):
1. Use FixedArray with pre-allocated capacity
2. Pack tokens into a flat Int array: positive = literal, negative = match"}
old_value:
{"id":"compress-claude-908","title":"Eliminate Token heap allocation in compressor","description":"The Array[Token] used to collect LZ77 output heap-allocates for every literal byte and match. For 10KB input this means ~10K allocations minimum. This is the primary cause of the 35-62% compression speed regression.

Fix options:
1. Use FixedArray with pre-allocated capacity (data.length() is an upper bound on token count)
2. Use two parallel FixedArrays (one for types, one for values) instead of enum
3. Pack tokens into a flat Int array: positive = literal byte, negative = -(length << 16 | dist) for matches
Option 3 avoids all enum/struct overhead and keeps data cache-friendly.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T11:05:27Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T11:05:27Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"Go's CRC-32 uses CLMUL (pclmulqdq) for hardware-accelerated IEEE CRC-32, not SSE4.2 _mm_crc32 (which computes CRC-32C, a different polynomial).

Two options for hardware acceleration via C FFI native stubs:

Option A: CRC-32C via SSE4.2 (simple)
- Direct use of _mm_crc32_u64 intrinsic
- ~20 lines of C
- Different polynomial than our current IEEE CRC-32
- Would need to add CRC-32C as a new function alongside existing CRC-32

Option B: IEEE CRC-32 via CLMUL (what Go does)
- Uses _mm_clmulepi64_si128 for carry-less multiplication
- ~100 lines of C (fold 128 bits at a time, Barrett reduction)
- Drop-in replacement for current software CRC-32
- This is what closes the 400x gap with Go

MoonBit C FFI approach:
- stub.c with #include <moonbit.h>, MOONBIT_FFI_EXPORT
- #borrow(data) extern "c" fn for zero-copy Bytes access
- moon.pkg: native-stub, targets gate ffi.mbt to native
- cc-flags: -mpclmul -msse4.2
- Runtime feature detection or compile-time #ifdef

Reference: https://www.moonbitlang.com/blog/moonbit-value-type for perf context
See moonbit-c-binding skill for full FFI patterns."}
old_value: {"id":"compress-claude-3rx","title":"Hardware-accelerated CRC-32 when available","description":"CRC-32 is 370x slower than Go because Go uses SSE4.2 CRC32C hardware instructions via assembly. MoonBit's slicing-by-8 is the best pure-software approach. When MoonBit gains SIMD/intrinsics support or native FFI on all targets, implement hardware-accelerated CRC-32. Current throughput: ~106 MB/s vs Go's ~40 GB/s.","acceptance_criteria":"Blocked on MoonBit SIMD/intrinsics support. Document the gap and track upstream progress.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:46Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:46Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"Hardware-accelerated Adler-32 via C FFI native stubs.

Go uses SSSE3/AVX2 to vectorize the s1/s2 accumulation using _mm_sad_epu8 for s1 sums and a weighted multiply-add for s2. This closes the 47x gap.

MoonBit C FFI approach:
- stub.c with SSSE3 intrinsics (_mm_sad_epu8, _mm_madd_epi16)
- Process 16 or 32 bytes per iteration with SIMD
- #borrow(data) extern "c" fn for zero-copy Bytes access  
- moon.pkg: native-stub, targets gate ffi.mbt to native
- cc-flags: -mssse3

Fallback: keep existing pure MoonBit 16x-unrolled implementation for non-native targets."}
old_value: {"id":"compress-claude-gs8","title":"Hardware-accelerated Adler-32 when available","description":"Adler-32 is 45x slower than Go because Go uses SIMD-accelerated implementation. MoonBit's unrolled loop is the best pure-software approach. Current throughput: ~103 MB/s vs Go's ~4.7 GB/s. Blocked on MoonBit SIMD/intrinsics support.","acceptance_criteria":"Blocked on MoonBit SIMD/intrinsics support. Document the gap and track upstream progress.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:51Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:51Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-908","title":"Eliminate Token heap allocation in compressor","description":"The Array[Token] used to collect LZ77 output heap-allocates for every literal byte and match. For 10KB input this means ~10K allocations minimum. This is the primary cause of the 35-62% compression speed regression.

Preferred approach: use #valtype with bit-packed Int64 to eliminate ALL heap allocation per token.

```moonbit
#valtype
struct PackedToken {
  val : Int64
}
```

Encoding scheme:
- Literal: val = byte_value (0-255, bit 32 clear)
- Match: val = (length << 16) | dist | (1 << 32) (bit 32 set = match flag)

This gives zero GC pressure since #valtype structs are stored inline/on-stack, not heap-allocated. See https://www.moonbitlang.com/blog/moonbit-value-type

Combined with a pre-allocated FixedArray[PackedToken] (data.length() is upper bound on token count), this should recover all the regression from the two-pass approach.

Previous options (still valid fallbacks):
1. Use FixedArray with pre-allocated capacity
2. Pack tokens into a flat Int array: positive = literal, negative = match","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T11:05:27Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T11:09:42Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-v2e","title":"Restore single-pass fast path for compression","description":"The two-pass token-collection approach for dynamic Huffman encoding caused a 35% regression in default compression speed (190.7µs → 255.7µs at 10KB). Every literal and match is heap-allocated into Array[Token], then iterated again to encode.

Fix: restore single-pass direct encoding as the fast path. Only use two-pass when dynamic blocks would actually help. Options:
1. Use single-pass fixed Huffman for levels 1-3 (speed-oriented), two-pass for levels 4+
2. Estimate whether dynamic blocks help based on data size (small blocks rarely benefit)
3. Inline token encoding: instead of Array[Token], use a flat FixedArray-based token buffer to avoid heap allocation per token","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T11:05:17Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T11:05:17Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-w6h","title":"Skip dynamic Huffman evaluation for small/fast blocks","description":"Even when the compressor chooses fixed Huffman blocks (which is common for small inputs), it still builds the full dynamic Huffman tree and calculates dynamic block size. This wastes time on:
1. Frequency counting over all tokens
2. Building Huffman tree from frequencies (sorting, tree construction)
3. Computing dynamic header size with RLE encoding
4. Comparing sizes

Fix: skip dynamic tree construction when it's unlikely to help:
- Blocks under ~1KB almost never benefit from dynamic Huffman
- Level 1 (BestSpeed) should always use fixed blocks
- Add a quick heuristic: if token count < threshold, skip dynamic evaluation","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T11:05:34Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T11:05:34Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-v3e","title":"Migrate precomputed lookup tables from FixedArray to ReadOnlyArray","description":"PLAN.md specifies ReadOnlyArray[T] for precomputed lookup tables but all 15+ tables use FixedArray or Array instead. ReadOnlyArray prevents accidental mutation and communicates immutability intent.

Tables to migrate:
- lib/checksum/crc32.mbt: crc32_tables (FixedArray[FixedArray[UInt]])
- lib/flate/deflate.mbt: fixed_lit_codes, fixed_dist_codes, length_encode_table, dist_encode_table
- lib/flate/types.mbt: length_base, length_extra, dist_base, dist_extra, code_order
- lib/flate/fixed_huffman.mbt: HuffmanDecoder chunks/links fields
- lib/bzip2/decompress.mbt: bzip2_crc_table
- lib/bzip2/types.mbt: block_magic, eos_magic (Array[Byte] → ReadOnlyArray[Byte])

Construct via ReadOnlyArray::from_array() or literal syntax. May need to update indexing if ReadOnlyArray uses different access patterns (it supports op_get/[]).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T11:14:44Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T11:14:44Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"PLAN.md is a reference design doc; actual implementation diverges intentionally (sync packages buffer-based, Token uses valtype, tables use ReadOnlyArray). Design improvements, not inaccuracies.","status":"closed"}
old_value:
{"id":"compress-claude-l8t","title":"PLAN.md inaccuracies to document","description":"Inaccuracies found in PLAN.md vs actual implementation:

1. Layer naming: PLAN.md says flate/async/ but code was initially flate/stream/ — now corrected
2. State machine sharing: PLAN.md assumes layers share state machines (Section 4). Actual implementation buffers all input in Layer 2/3 and calls Layer 1. Not truly streaming.
3. Token valtype: PLAN.md describes #valtype struct Token (Section 5a). Not implemented.
4. Bit pattern matching for headers: PLAN.md shows match data { [0x1Fu8, 0x8Bu8, ...] }. Actual code uses index-based parsing.
5. Test LOC gap: PLAN.md targets ~5,100 LOC of tests. Current: ~122 tests.
6. moonbitlang/core imports: Several core packages need explicit import — PLAN.md doesn't mention this.
7. bzip2 bench missing: PLAN.md lists bzip2_bench.mbt in bench/ but it was not created.
8. tools/ directory: PLAN.md describes tools/generate_golden.go, cross_validate.go, etc. — none exist yet.
9. testdata/ directory: PLAN.md expects testdata/e.txt, gettysburg.txt, pi.txt — not created.
10. Hasher trait: Implemented as planned but uses pub(open) trait not just pub trait.","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Review and update PLAN.md to reflect actual implementation decisions","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"chore","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T02:33:44Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T02:33:44Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-s20","title":"Sync packages are fake streaming - buffer everything, no constant-memory benefit","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T11:26:34Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T11:26:34Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"Sync packages removed; async packages already call L1 functions internally, no duplication remains.","status":"closed"}
old_value:
{"id":"compress-claude-091","title":"Shared state machines across layers","description":"Per PLAN.md Section 4, all three layers should share internal state machines (InflateState, DeflateState, etc.) rather than buffering entire input and calling Layer 1 functions.

Currently:
- Layer 2 (async) and Layer 3 (sync) both buffer all input then call Layer 1's compress()/decompress()
- This means streaming is not truly incremental — entire input is materialized in memory

Target:
- Internal InflateState/DeflateState with feed()/step() methods
- Layer 2 drives state machine with async I/O
- Layer 3 exposes state machine directly via update()/finish()
- Layer 1 wraps as compress(data) = state.write(data); state.finish()

This is a significant refactor but enables true streaming for large data.","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Internal state machine types (InflateState, DeflateState) exist in flate/
- [ ] Layer 3 sync uses state machine incrementally
- [ ] Layer 2 async drives state machine with async I/O
- [ ] Memory usage for streaming is O(window_size), not O(input_size)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T02:33:44Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T02:33:44Z"}

{"id":"compress-claude-gs8","title":"Hardware-accelerated Adler-32 when available","description":"Hardware-accelerated Adler-32 via C FFI native stubs.

Go uses SSSE3/AVX2 to vectorize the s1/s2 accumulation using _mm_sad_epu8 for s1 sums and a weighted multiply-add for s2. This closes the 47x gap.

MoonBit C FFI approach:
- stub.c with SSSE3 intrinsics (_mm_sad_epu8, _mm_madd_epi16)
- Process 16 or 32 bytes per iteration with SIMD
- #borrow(data) extern "c" fn for zero-copy Bytes access  
- moon.pkg: native-stub, targets gate ffi.mbt to native
- cc-flags: -mssse3

Fallback: keep existing pure MoonBit 16x-unrolled implementation for non-native targets.","acceptance_criteria":"Blocked on MoonBit SIMD/intrinsics support. Document the gap and track upstream progress.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"gbikal@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T10:31:51Z","created_by":"Bikal Gurung","updated_at":"2026-03-07T11:17:54Z"}
